attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man presents with a six week history of increasing numbness and clumsiness in his left hand. His hand feels weak when he is trying to use it to lift objects. The numbness mostly affects his left 1st and 2nd digits. He also has an intermittent sudden, sharp pain radiating from his left shoulder down the lateral aspect of his arm to his wrist. He has no speech disturbance, no vision changes and no headaches.

His medical history includes type 2 diabetes (on metformin single agent). His medications include metformin 500mg mane and magnesium 500mg BD. He is allergic to bandaids.

On examination he is alert and comfortable and oriented. His upper limbs appear symmetrical without wasting or fasciculations. He has reduced grip strength in the left hand. His reflexes on the left are: biceps 1+, brachioradialis 2+, triceps 2+. His reflexes on the right are: biceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, triceps 2+. He has reduced pinprick sensation on the left hand in the anatomical snuff box and on the lateral aspect of his shoulder.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following nerve roots is most likely to be involved?
attributes.explanation:
This question requires a good understanding of the brachial plexus. The five main peripheral nerves in the upper limb are: musculocutaneous nerve, axillary nerve, median nerve, radial nerve, and ulnar nerve.

  • The musculocutaneous nerve originates from C5-C7 nerve roots.

  • The axillary nerve originates from C5-C6 nerve roots.

  • The median nerve originates from C6-T1 nerve roots.

  • The radial nerve originates from C5-T1 nerve roots.

  • The ulnar nerve originates from C8-T1 nerve roots.

Associating this patient's symptoms and signs with possible nerve roots is the key to determining which nerve roots are likely to be involved. The key findings in the stem are are: numbness mostly in the 1st/2nd digits, radiating pain from left shoulder down the lateral aspect of the arm to the wrist, reduced grip strength, reduced biceps reflex, and reduced pinprick sensation on the left hand in the anatomical snuff box.

  • The numbness affecting the patients 1st and 2nd digits could be due to radial nerve damage, or due to median nerve damage.

  • The left shoulder sensory deficit and lateral arm and forearm sensory deficits (the radiating pain) are likely to be due to the axillary nerve and musculocutaneous nerves.

  • A 1+ bicep reflex could indicate musculocutaneous nerve damage, or could be a normal finding.

  • The sensory deficits around the anatomical 'snuff box' (the extensor surface between the 1st and 2nd digits) could be due to radial nerve damage.

  • Analysing all the symptoms and signs, the only common nerve roots are C5-C6, and therefore these are the most likely nerve roots affected.

C4-C5 nerve roots provide sensory innervation to the deltoid and lateral arm, but not the forearm, wrist or digits. This does not align with the man's symptoms.

C6-C7 nerve roots provide sensory innervation to the median anterior surface of the arm and forearm, the lateral posterior surface of the forearm, and the anterior and posterior surfaces of digits 1 to 4. The deltoid would be spared. This does not align with the man's symptoms.

C7-C8 nerve roots provide sensory innervation to the anterior and posterior surfaces of the 2nd- 5th digits, and the median portion of the posterior surface of the forearm. This does not align with the man's symptoms.

C8-T1 nerve roots provide sensory innervation to the 5th and part of the 4th digits and the medial anterior and posterior surfaces of the forearm and arm (1, 2). This does not align with the man's symptoms.
learningPoints: Associating this patient's symptoms and signs with possible nerve roots is the key to determining which nerve roots are likely to be involved.
A 1+ bicep reflex could indicate musculocutaneous nerve damage, or could be a normal finding
references: Polcaro L, Charlick M, Daly DT. Anatomy, Head and Neck, Brachial Plexus. [Updated 2021 Aug 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK531473/

Dydyk AM, Ngnitewe Massa R, Mesfin FB. Disc Herniation. [Updated 2022 Jan 18]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441822/

Walker HK. Deep Tendon Reflexes. In: Walker HK, Hall WD, Hurst JW, editors. Clinical Methods: The History, Physical, and Laboratory Examinations. 3rd edition. Boston: Butterworths; 1990. Chapter 72. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK396
